content.text: Based on the official Claude Code documentation and GitHub repositories,
in the Agent SDK Overview refers to
the two programming languages officially supported for building AI agents with the Claude Agent SDK
, enabling developers to leverage Claude Code's capabilities programmatically in their preferred language ecosystem.

Claude Agent SDK (formerly Claude Code SDK)
    ↓
Python SDK + TypeScript SDK
    ↓
Same tools, agent loop, context management
: "The Agent SDK gives you the same tools, agent loop, and context management that power Claude Code, programmable in Python and TypeScript".
Automatically bundled — no separate installation required
Full Python API reference and examples
Complete reference for all functions, types, and classes
first in your project directory, then
, so the package appears in your local node_modules and can be imported correctly".
Full TypeScript API reference and examples
Data science, backend automation, ML pipelines
Frontend integration, web services, real-time apps
reading files, running commands, editing code
Gather Context → Take Action → Verify Work → Repeat
File system for state management (Context Engineering)
: "The SDK also supports Claude Code's filesystem-based configuration. To use these features, set

from anthropics_agent_sdk import Agent

# Create agent that lists files
agent = Agent()
agent.list_files(".")
Integrate with NumPy, pandas, ML frameworks
Design system with software engineer, architect, SRE, tester agents
Think of Python and TypeScript support like
offering the same powerful car in two transmission types

: Python is automatic (simpler, more data science libraries), TypeScript is manual (more control, better for web development). The car (Agent SDK) is identical — same engine (Claude Code), same wheels (built-in tools), same steering (agent loop). You just choose the transmission based on your driving style (language preference).
Python & TypeScript = two officially supported languages for building AI agents with Claude Agent SDK
Same core SDK: same tools, agent loop, context management in both languages
Both support: built-in tools (files/commands/code editing), agent loop, context management, file-based config
Python use: backend automation, data science pipelines, ML model training
TypeScript use: frontend integration, web services, multi-agent workflows
Full API reference and examples available for both languages
Both support: multi-agent orchestration, custom tool integration (MCP), feedback loops, context engineering

The SDK is built directly on top of Claude Code because Anthropic found they were "rebuilding the same parts over and over again" for internal tools, exposing Claude Code's capabilities in both Python and TypeScript for developer flexibility.
Understanding the internals of python & typescript helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with python & typescript has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying python & typescript in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Python & TypeScript is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
Production experience with python & typescript has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.

Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.

Python & TypeScript is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Anthropic maintains first-party SDKs for Python and TypeScript/JavaScript, both tracking the same underlying API version. Python is the primary SDK in terms of feature completeness, documentation coverage, and community examples — new API capabilities appear in the Python SDK first, often weeks before TypeScript parity. The TypeScript SDK supports Node.js 18+ and modern browser environments via fetch-based HTTP, making it the natural choice for serverless functions, Next.js API routes, and frontend integrations. Both SDKs are MIT-licensed, published to PyPI and npm respectively, and accept contributions through GitHub. Anthropic uses semantic versioning and maintains a changelog for every release, so pinning a minor version in production is safe. Neither SDK supports synchronous blocking calls in async runtimes — both are fully async-native, which matters when choosing your architecture.
Python SDK: anthropic on PyPI, supports Python 3.8+, fully typed with mypy stubs, primary surface for new features.
TypeScript SDK: @anthropic-ai/sdk on npm, supports Node.js 18+ and modern browsers, ESM and CommonJS compatible.
Feature parity: both SDKs support streaming, tool use, vision, and prompt caching; the Python SDK tends to receive beta features first.
Versioning: both follow semver; breaking changes are in major versions only, so minor/patch upgrades are safe to apply.
TypeScript SDK provides generated TypeScript types for all request and response shapes, enabling IDE autocompletion without extra setup.
Neither SDK provides a synchronous blocking API — use asyncio in Python or async/await in TypeScript for all API calls.

The Python SDK exposes two client classes: Anthropic for synchronous-style code and AsyncAnthropic for use inside asyncio event loops. Despite the name, Anthropic is not truly synchronous in the blocking sense — it uses httpx under the hood and releases the GIL during I/O, making it safe to use in threaded applications. For async applications such as FastAPI or aiohttp servers, use AsyncAnthropic with await on every API call. The SDK provides a streaming context manager via client.messages.stream() that yields events as they arrive, allowing you to forward tokens to the user in real time without buffering the full response. Type stubs are included in the package, so mypy and pyright give full type coverage without additional packages. For agent workloads, the client is thread-safe and can be shared across threads or coroutines — create a single instance at application startup and reuse it throughout.
Anthropic class: synchronous-style client using httpx; suitable for scripts, CLI tools, and multi-threaded servers.
AsyncAnthropic class: for asyncio applications; use await client.messages.create(...) inside async def functions.
Streaming: async with client.messages.stream(...) as stream: async for text in stream.text_stream: yield text
Type checking: import anthropic and annotate variables with anthropic.MessageParam, anthropic.TextBlock, etc. for full mypy coverage.
Retry behavior: pass max_retries=3 to the constructor; the SDK uses exponential backoff with jitter on 429 and 529 errors.
Timeout configuration: client = Anthropic(timeout=60.0) sets a 60-second request timeout; set to None to disable.

The TypeScript SDK is designed for JavaScript-native async patterns and integrates naturally with frameworks like Next.js, Express, and Deno. Every method returns a Promise, and the SDK provides full TypeScript generics so response types narrow automatically based on request parameters. The streaming API uses async generators, compatible with both Node.js streams and the Web Streams API. One important constraint: the TypeScript SDK cannot be used in browser environments that lack CORS headers for api.anthropic.com — use it exclusively in server-side code (Node.js, Edge Runtime, Deno) or proxy requests through your own backend. The SDK also exports a toFile() helper that converts Buffer or ReadableStream to the File-like object required for vision API calls, smoothing over differences between Node.js and browser File APIs.
All methods return Promises; use await or .then() — there is no synchronous alternative.
TypeScript generics narrow response types: const response: Message = await client.messages.create({...})
Streaming: for await (const event of stream) {} using the built-in async generator interface.
Browser constraint: the SDK requires a CORS-enabled endpoint; use it only in Node.js, Edge Runtime, or Deno — never in untrusted browser code that exposes your API key.
toFile() helper: converts Node.js Buffer or ReadableStream to a File-compatible object for multipart requests.
ESM and CJS: the package ships both ESM and CommonJS builds; import from "@anthropic-ai/sdk" works in both module systems.
Deno support: import from "npm:@anthropic-ai/sdk" in Deno 1.28+ without any additional configuration.
Choosing the Right Language for Agent Development

The language choice for an agent codebase is typically driven by existing infrastructure rather than SDK feature differences. Python wins when your agents need to call machine learning libraries (PyTorch, scikit-learn, sentence-transformers), execute data processing pipelines with pandas or numpy, or run as standalone scripts or cron jobs. Python also has the richest ecosystem of agent frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, CrewAI) that build on the Anthropic SDK if you need higher-level abstractions. TypeScript wins when the agent runs inside an existing Node.js or Next.js application, needs to interact with browser APIs or DOM manipulation, integrates with npm packages for specific services, or deploys to Vercel Edge Functions or Cloudflare Workers where Python is not supported. For teams that are proficient in both, Python is generally the faster path to a working prototype because of the broader agent-specific documentation and examples.
Python: best for ML-adjacent tasks, data pipelines, scientific computing, and when leveraging the broader agent framework ecosystem.
TypeScript: best for web applications, serverless deployments on Vercel/Cloudflare, and when reusing existing Node.js service integrations.
Both languages: the agentic loop logic is identical conceptually — the SDK differences are surface-level syntactic, not architectural.
Team expertise matters most: a TypeScript team will ship faster with the TS SDK even if Python examples are more numerous online.
Mixed stacks: some teams write agent orchestration in Python and expose it via an HTTP API consumed by a TypeScript frontend — a clean separation of concerns.
Monorepo consideration: if your repo is already TypeScript, adding a Python agent service introduces build complexity; prefer the TS SDK unless Python is strictly required.
